and we have retracted the other side of the chest wall here.
So what you're seeing here is the interior of the anterior chest wall.
So let's show a few quick salient points here.
This shiny surface that you see here on both the sides, this is the parietal pleura, the costal part of the parietal pleura which is lining the interior of the chest wall.
This parietal part of the costal pleura
continues all along the chest wall and then it goes and gets reflected inside along the vertebral line of pleural reflection and it becomes continuous with the mediastinal pleura.
So that line of pleural reflection is referred to as the costovertebral pleural reflection where my hand is moving right now.
This same costal part of the parietal pleura continues down and it gets reflected on the dome of the diaphragm.
This is the right dome of the diaphragm.
Similarly, on this side, this is the left dome of the diaphragm.
And that is called the costal reflection.
And this recess is referred to as the costodiaphragmatic recess.

And finally, the same parietal pleura, the costal part of the parietal pleura will get reflected from the sternum onto the anterior surface of the mediastinum along the sternal part of pleural reflection and this recess is called the costomediastinal recess.
Inside the costomediastinal recess will be fitting the anterior margin of the lung.
You can see this is the anterior margin of the lung.
The lung is highly shriveled and this is the anterior margin of the left lung.

the costodiaphragmatic greases where my hand is located right now will be fitting the inferior margin of the lung and finally on the posterior most vertebral greases will be fitting the blunt posterior surface of the lung the posterior border of the lung so these two these are the two lungs so this space that you see here which is highly exaggerated in both these cadavers both these sides these are the pleural spaces

So the pleural space essentially is the small space between the visceral pleura which is covering the surface of the lung and the parietal pleura which is covering the inner surface of the chest wall.
Let's proceed with a few other points.
If you look very carefully on the inner surface of both the sides, and I'm going to show each side, you can see this one blood vessel, one prominent blood vessel running all the way down.
This is the internal thoracic artery.
This internal thoracic artery is the one which provides

the anterior intercostal arteries and we can see a shadow of anterior intercostal artery here anterior intercostal artery similarly on the other side we can see this vessel running down this is the other side internal thoracic artery or the internal mammary artery and this is also going to give the anterior intercostal artery these anterior intercostal arteries there will be two we need space
Up to the 6th space, 7th, 8th and 9th space will be provided by the musculoprenic artery.
These anterior intercostal arteries will run and they will anastomose with the posterior intercostal arteries and their collaterals which come from the thoracic aorta which we cannot see.
And through this parietal pleura we can see the innermost intercostal muscle.
You note that the parietal pleura and the visceral pleura are highly shiny and they are closely adherent to the surface of the chest wall and the surface of the lung respectively.
However, if we strip off this parietal pleura a little bit as I am doing right now, you can see a connective tissue underneath.
That is the endothoracic fascia.

and the artery is running in the endothoracic fascia covered by the parietal pleura so therefore just external to the parietal pleura on both the sides will be the layer of endothoracic fascia the endothoracic fascia has also got the same components it has got the costal part

The diaphragmatic part, which covers the diaphragm, under the parietal pleura, it's got the mediastinal part, and there will be a small portion above the first rib, which you cannot see here, which is called the cervical part, also referred to as the suprapleural membrane or the Simpson's fasciae.
Now let's take a look at what else can we see in this particular dissection.
I have not yet removed the fascia and the parietal pleura from the mediastinum, which is going to be part of the next dissection.

But what we can see is that apart from the fact that the lung is highly shriveled and contracted here, but we can make out this is the oblique fascia of the lung, right lung, and this is the horizontal fascia.
And if you retract it laterally, we can see it is attached to the mediastinum but through this structure here.
This is the hilum of the right lung.
Similarly, the left lung is also highly shriveled and contracted and you can see this is the anterior margin.
This has got only this fissure here.
This is the oblique fissure.
Therefore, the right lung has got three lobes, a superior lobe, a middle lobe and inferior lobe.
The left lung has got only a superior lobe and an inferior lobe.
And on the left side also, we can see, if I retract it laterally, we can see this structure here.
This is the hilum of the lung.

So the point to be remembered here is that the parietal pleura, as it goes, becomes the mediastinal pleura, it comes to the hilum of the lung, and it gets reflected onto the surface of the lung as the visceral pleura.
So therefore, at the hilum of the lung on both the sides, the parietal pleura becomes continuous with the visceral pleura.
That is the point to be remembered.
And if I were to reflect it further laterally, we can see a double fold of peritoneum hanging down from here, from the inferior margin of the hilum of the lung.
That double fold of peritoneum is referred to as the pulmonary ligament.
What does this pulmonary ligament do?
It attaches the lung to the mediastinum, number one.
And secondly, it prevents torsion of the lung.
So therefore, that is a function of the pulmonary ligament on either side.
And before I conclude, I would like to show you, we have just removed a little bit of the fascia and the parietal glura from the mediastinum to show you the outline of the heart.
So this is the heart inside the pericardial cavity.
But we shall do a further dissection of the heart at a later stage and that is for a later dissection.
Whenever we have any fluid collection in this, as you can see, we have poured some fluid here.
This is something which is very similar to what is known as hydrothorax or pleural effusion.
Similarly, in that side also, any collection and that is called hydrothorax or pleural effusion.
So, when there is such a situation, we have to do what is known as pleurosynthesis.

We count the second, third, second space, third, fourth, fifth space and in the mid-axillary line, we insert a needle and we put in a chest tube and we connect it to an underwater seal drainage and that is known as pleurosynthesis.
plurosynthesis, or thoracentesis.
That is the procedure to deal with hydrothorax, pneumothorax, hemothorax, or hydronemothorax, or any combination of these conditions.
